You are asked to see a 45-year-old man with a history of type 2 diabetes mellitus for routine follow-up. He feels well and has no acute concerns. You have been encouraging his efforts at lifestyle changes with the hope of achieving some weight loss. His weight of 244 lb (110.9 kg) has been stable over the past year. He is otherwise healthy. He comments that he was relieved when he recently heard on the news that lifestyle changes do not really help people with type 2 diabetes, and he asks whether he must continue his activity program of walking 30 minutes to 5 times per week.

He currently takes metformin, 2000 mg daily; simvastatin, 20 mg daily; and lisinopril, 20 mg daily.

On physical examination, he appears well. Height is 70 in (177.8 cm), and weight is 244 lb (110.9 kg) (BMI = 35 kg/m $ ^{2} $). Blood pressure is 138/80 mm Hg.

Laboratory test results:

Hemoglobin  $ A_{lc} = 6.8\% $ (0.068)

Serum creatinine = 1.0 mg/dL (88.4 μmol/L)

In this patient, intensive lifestyle changes (goals of weight loss of >7% and 175 minutes of moderate-intensity activity per week) would be expected to decrease which of the following?
options: Risk of developing diabetic microvascular disease
items.explanation: Correct answer: A.

A 31-year-old man has a history of Rathke cleft cyst treated with transsphenoidal surgery. He presents for follow-up of his persistent anterior pituitary failure and review of his hormone replacement regimen. He is doing well, but he is concerned about ongoing weight gain. His thyroid hormone replacement dosage was adjusted several times, but it has now been stable for the past 6 months. He otherwise has no concerns. He has been following a calorie-counting weight-loss program and has lost about 4.4 lb (2 kg) in the last month. He has instituted an exercise program and visits the gym 3 times per week to participate in both aerobic and resistance exercises. Weight history:
MeasurementTime Point
High SchoolBefore Transsphenoidal SurgeryToday
Weight268 lb (121.8 kg)286 (130 kg)321 lb (145.9 kg)
BMI34 $ kg/m^{{2}} $36 $ kg/m^{{2}} $41.2 $ kg/m^{{2}} $
Medications include testosterone gel, 20.25/1.25 g, 3 pumps applied to the chest and shoulders topically daily; hydrocortisone, 15 mg in the morning and 5 mg in the evening; levothyroxine, 125 mcg daily; and vitamin D $ ^{3} $, 1000 IU daily. On physical examination, he appears well. Blood pressure is 120/65 mm Hg. His thyroid gland is normal. He does not have striae on skin examination, his obesity is primarily central in distribution, and his muscle strength is normal. Testicular volume is 12 mL bilaterally. Laboratory test results: TSH = 0.9 mIU/L Bioavailable testosterone = 179 ng/dL (6.2 nmol/L) Free $ T_{4} $ = 0.9 ng/dL (11.6 pmol/L) $$ GH=0.04ng/mL(0.04\mu g/L) $$ Total testosterone = 416 ng/dL (14.4 nmol/L) He undergoes indirect calorimetry, which reveals a measured basal metabolic rate of 1844 kcal. This corresponds to 69% of predicted basal caloric requirements on the basis of his age, height, and weight. Which of the following is the best next step to facilitate this patient's weight loss efforts?
items.explanation:
## 25 ANSWER: B) Increase his levothyroxine dosage This

25 A This patient is concerned about continued weight gain. One potential explanation would be excessive glucocorticoid replacement, which can lead to multiple adverse effects including lower bone mineral density, lower lean muscle mass, and weight gain. Adequacy of supplementation is often determined by subjective clinical parameters that are nonspecific. In patients with cortisol deficiency who take what is deemed to be appropriate replacement therapy, scores in validated self-assessment surveys of wellness (SF36) are consistently lower than scores of age- and sex-matched control participants. The scores are lowest in patients with a central cause for adrenal insufficiency. However, in view of this patient's height and weight, his current steroid dosage is most appropriate and further reductions are not advised.

Testosterone deficiency is associated with changes in body composition such as increases in percentage of body fat and decreases in lean muscle mass. Replacement of gonadal steroids can improve lean muscle mass. However, this intervention alone is not expected to contribute to weight loss (thus, Answer E is incorrect). GH deficiency also contributes to unfavorable changes in body composition (decreased lean muscle mass). However, the decision to initiate GH supplementation is rarely related to weight management (thus, Answer C is incorrect).

A basal metabolic rate measurement via indirect calorimetry determines basal caloric requirements and can guide dietary therapy when patients attempt to lose weight. Because thyroid hormone is a determinant of energy expenditure, measurement of the basal metabolic rate can also provide information regarding the adequacy of thyroid hormone supplementation. This patient's resting energy expenditure was accurately measured. He achieved a steady state at 1844 kcal, representing 69% of his predicted values on the basis of his age, height, weight. Values less than 20% of predicted are consistent with a hypometabolic state. His levothyroxine dosage was therefore increased (Answer B is correct) to 150 mcg daily and subsequently to 175 mcg daily. In addition, regular physical activity, he was advised to limit his caloric intake to 1800 to 2200 kcal daily. Over the subsequent 6 months, he was able to lose 26.5 lb (12 kg). He felt well and motivated to continue his efforts. Another measurement of resting energy expenditure was planned in 6 to 12 months.

Proponents of combined $T_{4}$ and $T_{3}$ supplementation comment on improved patient well-being, yet there are data to support a weight benefit with this combination (thus. Answer D is incorrect).

DHEA is a steroid intermediate in the pathway of adrenal and gonadal steroid biosynthesis. DHEA supplements have been purported to improve a wide array of symptoms including fatigue, muscle strength, and reproductive function. However, it would not facilitate weight loss and would be more likely to be weight-neutral or cause weight gain (thus, Answer A is incorrect).

##### Educational Objective:

Assess the adequacy of a hormone supplementation regimen in a patient with panhypopituitarism and weight

UpToDate Topic Review(s):

Obesity in adults: Overview of management

##### Reference(s):

Ferretti E, Persani L, Jaffrain-Rea ML, Giambona S, Tamburrano G, Beck-Peccoz P. Evaluation of the adequacy of levothyroxine replacement therapy in with central hypothyroidism. J Clin Endocrinol Metab. 1999;84(3):924-929.

Hoffman AR, Kuntze JE, Baptista J, et al. Growth hormone (GH) replacement therapy in adult-onset GH deficiency: effects on body composition in men's women in a double-blind, randomized, placebo-controlled trial. J Clin Endocrinol Metab. 2004;89(5):2048-2056.

Bhasin S, Cunningham GR, Hayes

items.question:
A 56-year-old African American man seeks your advice regarding his cardiovascular risk. He is adopted and knows no details about his family history. He has hypertension controlled on 2 drugs. He has no other conditions. He does not smoke cigarettes and has no known cardiovascular disease. His primary care is to reduce risk. He is a risk for a cardiovascular disease event and recommended that he take a statin to reduce risk. He is coming to you for a second opinion. He states he is reasonably physically active, eats a balanced food, feels healthy. He is willing to take medication if it will help reduce his risk of heart disease, but he is not a risk.

physical examination, blood pressure is 133/82 mm Hg (while on his medications). Height is 66 in (167.6 weight is 170 lb (77.3 kg) (BMI = 27.4 kg/m $ ^{2} $). A full examination reveals no abnormalities.

Laboratory test results (fasting):

Glucose = 105 mg/dL (5.83 mmol/L)

HDL cholesterol = 42 mg/dL (1.09 mmol/L)

Hemoglobin  $ A_{1c}=5.9\% $ (0.059)

LDL cholesterol = 155 mg/dL (4.01 mmol/L)

Total cholesterol = 202 mg/dL (5.23 mmol/L)

Triglycerides = 95 mg/dL (1.07 mmol/L)

His 10-year cardiovascular risk is estimated at 16% using the Adult Treatment Panel III calculator, or 12.9% (lifetime risk 50%) using the American Heart Association/American College of Cardiology risk calculator.

In this patient, starting statin therapy is:
options: Indicated and should be initiated now
Indicated and should be initiated now, but in addition to a fibrate
Contraindicated because of the risk of causing myopathy
Contraindicated because of the risk of causing hemorrhagic stroke
Contraindicated because of the risk of causing overt diabetes mellitus
items.explanation: Correct answer: A.

items.question:
A 63-year-old man has recently moved to your city and presents to establish care. He has a history of hypertension and had a myocardial infarction 2 years ago. He has no angina or shortness of breath. He is moderately active and walks daily. He does not smoke cigarettes. Current medications include aspirin, metoprolol, and lisinopril. You note that he is not on a statin, but he reports that he previously tried simvastatin, atorvastatin, rosuvastatin, and ezetimibe + simvastatin, but had intolerable myalgias while taking each of these.

On physical examination, pulse rate is 84 beats/min and blood pressure is 128/84 mm Hg. Height is 66 in (167.6 cm), and weight is 220 lb (100 kg) (BMI = 35.5 kg/m $ ^{2} $). Physical examination findings are normal.

Laboratory test results (fasting):

Glucose = 115 mg/dL (6.38 mmol/L)

Hemoglobin  $ A_{lc}=6.3\% $ (0.063)

HDL cholesterol = 28 mg/dL (0.73 mmol/L)

Total cholesterol = 138 mg/dL (3.57 mmol/L)

LDL cholesterol = 75 mg/dL (1.94 mmol/L)

Triglycerides = 280 mg/dL (3.16 mmol/L)

In addition to recommending a calorie-restricted diet, which of the following should you prescribe?
items.explanation:
## 41 ANSWER: B) Fenofibrate

Despite his low LDL-cholesterol level, this man has already experienced a myocardial infarction and is at very high risk for further cardiovascular disease events. He does not smoke cigarettes, his blood pressure is controlled, and he is taking appropriate cardiovascular medications (aspirin,  $ \beta $-adrenergic blocker, ACE inhibitor). Thus, the only other modifiable cardiovascular risk factor is his dyslipidemia. Most guidelines recommend statins in patients with established cardiovascular disease to further reduce risk; the Adult Treatment Panel III (ATP

III) guidelines recommend treatment to achieve an LDL-cholesterol concentration less than 100 mg/dL (<2.59 mmol/L) with an optional goal of less than 70 mg/dL (<1.81 mmol/L). The newer American Heart Association American College of Cardiology guidelines have moved away from target-driven therapy, and instead recom割high-intensity statin therapy to further reduce risk in patients at high risk such as this one, regardless of LDL-cholesterol level. This recommendation is based in part on studies such as Treat to New Targets (TNT), which compared high-dosage vs low-dosage atorvastatin and found further reductions in events in participants with low LDL-cholesterol levels. However, this patient has had intolerable myalgias with at least 3 statins. Although he not tried pravastatin (Answer A), the fact that he had myalgias with atorvastatin, simvastatin, and rosuvastatin, which differ in their relative hydrophilicity, hepatic metabolism, clearance, and elimination half-life, suggests the will most likely not tolerate pravastatin either. The prevalence of muscle symptoms in statin users is reported to be 5% to 10%; however, the placebo groups generally have a similar prevalence of muscle symptoms, which reflects the high population prevalence of muscle symptoms. In general, if a patient has myalgias to one statin, trying another statin with different pharmacokinetic profiles is appropriate. However, if several have been tried they each cause the same reaction, a tolerable statin is unlikely to be found. A growing body of literature address intermittent statin dosing (such as alternate day) to increase statin tolerability; however, this has not yet been tested with respect to cardiovascular outcomes.

Although statins have the best evidence to support their reduction of cardiovascular disease events, other lip lowering agents can also improve outcomes. Ezetimibe (Answer D) is a cholesterol absorption inhibitor that low LDL cholesterol via a different mechanism than statins. Although this patient previously reported myalgias when taking a statin + ezetimibe combination pill, the symptoms were almost certainly due to the statin component. It would most likely tolerate ezetimibe as monotherapy. However, to date, ezetimibe has not been shown to decrease risk of cardiovascular events. Given that this patient's main lipid abnormalities are low HDL cholesterol and triglycerides, and his LDL-cholesterol level is already fairly low, further LDL lowering with an agent that has not yet been shown to improve outcomes is not the best choice.

Fish oil (Answer C) has been used for its triglyceride-lowering effects, but it is usually recommended for severe hypertriglyceridemia—when triglyceride concentrations are greater than 500 mg/dL (>5.65 mmol/L) and there is a risk of pancreatitis. Fish oil has not been shown to reduce cardiovascular events.

Fibrates (Answer B) have been studied in a variety of monotherapy trials for both primary and secondary prevention. Although fibrates have minimal effects on LDL cholesterol, they can lower triglycerides by up to 50% and raise HDL-cholesterol levels by 10% to 15%. In several studies, including the VA-HIT trial, fibrates reduced cardiovascular events. Similarly, niacin (Answer E), which can lower triglycerides and LDL cholesterol and raise HDL cholesterol, reduced recurrent cardiovascular events and mortality in the Coronar

items.question:
A 64-year-old woman (a current patient) calls your office with concerns about her medications. She recently started a new diet that is heavy in fruits, including a daily glass of grapefruit for breakfast every day. She recalls the pharmacist telling her that she cannot take statins if she eats grapefruit. She had a myocardial infarction followed by coronary artery bypass grafting 3 years ago and she has since been taking atorvastatin, 40 mg daily. She has hypertension controlled with metoprolol. She is obese but is otherwise healthy. At her last visit, weight was 186 lb (84.5 kg) and height was 64 in (162.6 cm) (BMI = 31.9 kg/m $ ^{2} $).

Recent laboratory test results (fasting) while on atorvastatin (but before starting the grapefruit diet):

Total cholesterol = 142 mg/dL (3.68 mmol/L)

Triglycerides = 98 mg/dL (1.11 mmol/L)

HDL cholesterol = 42 mg/dL (1.09 mmol/L)

Creatinine = 0.7 mg/dL (61.9 μmol/L)

LDL cholesterol = 69 mg/dL (1.79 mmol/L)

Alanine aminotransferase = 28 U/L (0.47 μkat/L)

Which of the following should be your advice?
options: Stop the statin and start a fibrate
Stop the statin and start ezetimibe
Continue the current statin at the same dosage
items.explanation:
## 52 ANSWER: E) Continue the current statin at the same dosage 52 The issue in this vignette is the safety of statin use in patients who consume grapefruit (fruit or juice). The lay literature is ripe with danger warnings about the consumption of grapefruit while taking statins, and patients are routinely counseled to avoid grapefruit or its juice when taking certain medications, including statins. Grapefruit and its juice contain a number of compounds that irreversibly inhibit CYP3A enzymes that are key in the metabolism of a number of drugs. Most statins undergo significant metabolism by the CYP3A system; thus, if the enzymes are inhibited, this leads to increased systemic plasma concentrations of statins, which has the potential to increase clinical and adverse effects. Because the CYP3A system is irreversibly inhibited, this can have effects on drug clearance for the several days after even a single dose. However, the impact of inhibiting CYP3A on statin metabolism is related to the clearance pathway and bioavailability of the specific agent. For example, simvastatin undergoes extensive first-line metabolism by CYP3A4, so its mean oral bioavailability is about 5%; inhibition of CYP3A4 could lead to significant increases—up to 10-fold—in its bioavailability. Conversely, atorvastatin has a higher mean oral bioavailability of 15% to 30%, so concentrations are only modestly increased by CYP3A inhibitors. Another consideration is how the grapefruit or juice does it take to have a significant impact on CYP3A? Several small pharmacokinetic studies suggest that it takes more than 500 mL of grapefruit juice on a daily basis to affect drug metabolism. Consumption of 600 to 750 mL of grapefruit juice each day has been shown to increase the plasma concentration of atorvastatin by 1.3-fold. However, it is quite rare to consume that much grapefruit or grapefruit juice on a regular basis. One study evaluated more commonly consumed amounts: one 300-mL (10-oz) glass of grapefruit juice per day for 90 days in long-term atorvastatin users. Participants were randomly assigned to continue their usual atorvastatin dosage or to decrease their baseline dosage by 50% when starting the grapefruit juice. Participants who continued their baseline dosage had an increase in atorvastatin plasma concentrations of up to 26% compared with concentrations at baseline with negligible changes in lipid levels and no adverse effects on liver function or serum creatine phosphokinase values. Participants randomly assigned to decrease their baseline dosage by 50% had a decrease in plasma atorvastatin concentrations of 12% to 25%, with an increase in LDL-cholesterol concentration (9%) and triglycerides (23%) compared with baseline concentrations. Again, no adverse effects on liver function or creatine phosphokinase levels were observed. Thus, on the basis of this study's findings, the Answer E is correct—no change in statin dosage is needed. The prescribing information raises concern with “excessive grapefruit juice consumption >1.2 liters/day.” If this patient had any other risk factors such as use of other C. some calcium channel blockers, some macrolide antibiotics), were frail, or were taking multiple medications, then decreasing the statin dosage (Answer B) might be reasonable. Given this patient's high risk for a recurrent cardiovascular event, discontinuing the statin, even if starting an alternative agent such as a fibrate or ezetimibe, would not be appropriate (thus, Answers A, C, D are incorrect). It is important to note that different statins are subject to different metabolism, and thus the response to grapefruit juice or other inhibitors of CYP3A4 is agent specific. Most studies evaluating the effect of grapefruit� statins are relatively short term (see Table).
Table. Effects of Grapefruit on Statin Metabolism.

items.question:
A 54-year-old woman is seen for follow-up after undergoing Roux-en-Y gastric bypass 6 months ago to experience remission of her impaired fasting hyperglycemia, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and obstructive sleep apnea. Her preoperative weight was 306.5 lb (139.3 kg), and her weight today was 224 lb (101.8 kg).

Two weeks ago, she developed severe back and groin pain for which she went to her local emergency department. After testing and x-rays, she was told she had kidney stones. You review the emergency department records that document a urinalysis with hematuria (no other abnormalities) and a kidneys-ureter-bladder radiograph showing a radiopaque stone. She has no personal or family history of nephrolithiasis.

Her current medications are calcium carbonate, 750-mg chewable tablet, 2 tablets by mouth twice daily; vitamin B $ _{12} $, 1000 mcg subcutaneously once monthly; a children's chewable multivitamin, twice daily; and vitamin D $ _{3} $, 2000 IU once daily.

On physical examination, BMI is $37 \, \text{kg/m}^2$. Her blood pressure is $104/84 \, \text{mm Hg}$, and pulse rate is $84 \, \text{beats/min}$. The rest of her examination findings are unremarkable.

You order the laboratory testing recommended by her bariatric surgery program:

Complete blood cell count, normal Lipid profile, normal

Ferritin, normal Serum calcium = 9.7 mg/dL (2.4 mmol/L)

Blood glucose (fasting), normal 25-Hydroxyvitamin D = 28 ng/mL (69.9 nmol/L)

You next order a 24-hour urine supersaturation profile. An increase in which of the following is the most likely finding?
items.explanation:
## 58 ANSWER: D) Calcium oxalate crystals

Enteric hyperoxaluria and calcium oxalate stone formation are well-recognized complications of malabsorptive bariatric operations such as the jejunoileal bypass. Until recently, the risk for hyperoxaluria and calcium oxalate stone formation with modern bariatric surgery, mainly Roux-en-Y gastric bypass, was unrecognized. Investigators have documented this association and report almost a doubling of the risk for the development of renal stone disease when compared with the risk of obese control participants. However, the overall risk for stone formation appears to be low even in patients with a history of renal stone disease and should not represent a contraindication to bariatric surgery. Ongoing investigation and surveillance is advised to identify patients.

The risk for renal stone disease is highest during the first 5 years after Roux-en-Y gastric bypass. The most common laboratory abnormalities reported include hyperoxaluria (>100 mg/24 h urine specimen), low urinary citrate (<500 mg/24 h urine specimen) (thus, Answer B is incorrect), and low urine volumes (<1.5 L) (thus, Answer E is incorrect). Patients with markedly low urine volumes (<1 L) can have substantial elevations in calcium oxalate supersaturations despite minimal abnormalities in urinary oxalate and citrate excretion. Although obesity is associated with higher risk for uric acid renal stones, the risks drops significantly after bariatric surgery and uric acid excretion is low (thus, Answer C is incorrect). Calcium excretion varies depending on dietary calcium intake and calcium supplementation, but it is rarely elevated (thus, Answer A is incorrect).

Increased calcium oxalate supersaturation was the most common abnormality reported in study examining a patient cohort after Roux-en-Y gastric bypass (thus, Answer D is correct). A proposed mechanism for the observed hyperoxaluria is increased intestinal absorption of oxalate with diets low in calcium and high in fat. As with any renal stone disease, low urine volumes increase the risk. For at-risk patients, recommendations include sufficient fluid intake to maintain a urine volume greater than 2 L daily, restriction of dietary fat, and adequate calcium intake either from dietary sources or supplementation.

##### Educational Objective:

Predict characteristics of the urine supersaturation profile in patients at risk for calcium oxalate renal stones after Roux-en-Y gastric bypass.

UpToDate Topic Review(s):

Risk factors for calcium stones in adults

##### Reference(s):

Sinha MK, Collazo-Clavell ML, Rule A, et al. Hyperoxaluric nephrolithiasis is a complication of Roux-en-Y gastric bypass surgery. Kidney Int. 2007;72(1):100-107.181(6):2573-2577

Matlaga BR, Shore AD. Magnuson T. Clark JM. Johns R, Makary MA. Effect of gastric bypass surgery on kidney stone disease. J Urol. 2009;181(6):2573-2577.

Park AM, Storm DW, Fulmer BR, Still CD, Wood GC, Hartle JE 2nd. A prospective study of risk factors for nephrolithiasis after Roux-en-Y gastric bypass surgery. J Urol. 2009;182(5):2334-2339.

Enteric hyperoxaluria and calcium oxalate stone formation are well-recognized complications of malabsorptive bariatric operations such as the jejunoileal bypass. Investigators have documented this association and report almost a doubling of the risk for the development of renal stone disease when compared with the risk of obese control participants. However, the overall risk for stone formation appears to be low even in patients with a history of renal stone disease and should not represent a contraindication to bariatric surgery. Ongoing investigation and surveillance is advised to identify patients.

The risk for renal stone disease is highest during the first 5 years after Roux-en-Y gastric bypass. The most common laboratory abnormalities reported include hyperoxaluria (>100 mg/24 h urine specimen), low urinary citrate (<500 mg/24 h urine specimen) (thus, Answer B is incorrect), and low urine volumes (<1.5 L) (thus, Answer E is incorrect). Patients with markedly low urine volumes (<1 L) can have substantial elevations in calcium oxalate supersaturations despite minimal abnormalities in urinary oxalate and citrate excretion. Although obesity is associated with higher risk for uric acid renal stones, the risks drops significantly after bariatric surgery and uric acid excretion is low (thus, Answer C is incorrect). Calcium excretion varies depending on dietary calcium intake and calcium supplementation, but it is rarely elevated (thus, Answer A is incorrect).

Increased calcium oxalate supersaturation was the most common abnormality reported in study examining a patient cohort after Roux-en-Y gastric bypass (thus, Answer D is correct). A proposed mechanism for the observed hyperoxaluria is increased intestinal absorption of oxalate with diets low in calcium and high in fat. As with any renal stone disease, low urine volumes increase the risk. For at-risk patients, recommendations include sufficient fluid intake to maintain a urine volume greater than 2 L daily, restriction of dietary fat, and adequate calcium intake either from dietary sources or supplementation.

##### Educational Objective:

Predict characteristics of the urine supersaturation profile in patients at risk for calcium oxalate renal stones after Roux-en-Y gastric bypass.

A 38-year-old biologic male patient presents to discuss transgender hormone therapy. The patient reports a lifelong feeling of having been born in the wrong body, and she struggled with anxiety and depression in her teenage years and 20s. She states that she drank alcohol heavily in the past, but quit completely after she had an episode of pancreatitis in her late 20s. She struggles with her weight. She recently sought psychological evaluation, and her psychologist concluded that she has gender dysphoria. At today's appointment, the patient requests estrogen therapy, and she presents a letter from her psychologist confirming the diagnosis of gender dysphoria and stating that the patient is ready to transition. On physical examination, height is 71 in (180.3 cm) and weight is 215 lb (97.7 kg) (BMI = 30.0 kg/m $ ^{2} $). Blood pressure is 136/86 mm Hg, and pulse rate is 72 beats/min. Physical examination findings are otherwise normal. You counsel the patient regarding potential adverse effects of medications and then prescribe transdermal estradiol and spironolactone. Two months later, she returns for follow-up assessment.

In addition to counseling regarding a low-fat diet, which of the following is the
options: Change the route of estrogen administration to oral delivery
items.explanation:
## 67 ANSWER: C) Prescribe a fibrate

Elevated triglycerides can occur in susceptible individuals who are prescribed estrogen therapy, and management of this problem can be difficult. Because this patient has had an episode of pancreatitis, which may or may not be alcohol related, she is at relatively high risk for another episode, and watchful waiting or dietary control as the sole strategy is not appropriate. This patient's estradiol level is barely out of the normal male range, thus, increasing her estradiol dosage would be appropriate and this could further elevate triglycerides. In addition to dietary counseling, medication changes are indicated.

If discontinuing estrogen is an option (Answer A) (for example, in a young woman taking an oral contraceptive, an alternative form of birth control may be reasonable), then this should be done. However, because there is no other treatment given this patient's goals, it is unlikely that she would be willing to discontinue estrogen. At this point, she has had no adverse medical events or contraindications to therapy, so discontinuing estradiol therapy is incorrect. The route of estrogen delivery (oral, transdermal, intramuscular) has been studied for effects on estrogen-related complications. Transdermal estrogen avoids hepatic first-pass metabolism and tends to have less induction of hepatic protein synthesis, particularly inflammatory, coagulation, and fibrinolysis proteins, than oral estrogen. Similarly, transdermal estrogen is associated with less elevation of HDL cholesterol and triglycerides than oral estrogen, but this effect varies among individuals. Given that this patient has a history of pancreatitis and substantial triglyceride elevations while taking estrogen, changing the delivery route (Answer B) not sufficient to reduce her risk and could potentially increase her triglyceride elevations.

A 54-year-old man makes an appointment to discuss hyperlipidemia (self-referral). He has a history of focal segmental glomerulosclerosis causing progressive renal failure and he recently started hemodialysis. His nephrologist has been counseling him regarding his elevated risk for cardiovascular disease and advising him about risk reduction. He is being assessed for possible renal transplant. He does not smoke cigarettes. He has longstanding hypertension (controlled on 3 medications) and a family history of cardiovascular disease in his father and paternal uncle who had myocardial infarctions at age 52 years and 48 years, respectively.

On physical examination, height is 73 in (185.4 cm) and weight is 195 lb (88.6 kg) (BMI = 25.7 kg/m $ ^{2} $). Blood pressure is 128/64 mm Hg, and pulse rate is 74 beats/min. Examination findings are abnormal only for a vascular thrill in his left arm where he has an arteriovenous fistula.

Laboratory test results (fasting):

Total cholesterol = 200 mg/dL (5.18 mmol/L)

LDL cholesterol = 158 mg/dL (4.09 mmol/L)

HDL cholesterol = 32 mg/dL (0.83 mmol/L)

Triglycerides = 176 mg/dL (1.99 mmol/L)

He wants to know whether he should start a statin to reduce his cardiovascular risk. Which of the following should you recommend?
options: Start a statin; he will have the same relative risk reduction as that of a patient with normal renal function
Start a statin; however, his relative risk reduction is less than that of a patient without chronic kidney disease
Start a statin; specifically, simvastatin—no other statin has been shown to reduce cardiovascular risk in patients with chronic kidney disease
Do not start a statin; it is contraindicated in this patient
Do not start a statin; it has no cardiovascular benefit in this patient
items.explanation: Correct answer: A.

items.question:
A 66-year-old man is interested in bariatric surgery. He has a longstanding history of being obese despite multiple weight-loss attempts. He is currently at his heaviest weight of 338 lb (153.6 kg). He has difficulty adhering to dietary recommendations. He struggles with enforcing portion control, making healthful choices, and managing hunger and cravings. Three years ago, he lost weight in preparation for total knee arthroplasty (left knee), which was complicated by deep venous thrombosis. Unfortunately, he regained the weight initially lost. Over the past year, he has been more limited in his activity because of symptomatic osteoarthritis involving his right knee. He has been advised that he will need a total knee arthroplasty for his right knee, but that he is not eligible at his current weight. He experienced a myocardial infarction 6 years ago, but he currently has no active angina symptoms and at his most recent cardiac assessment, he was not advised to make any changes to his program. Other health concerns include hypertension, hyperlipidemia, impaired fasting hyperglycemia, and obstructive sleep apnea for which he uses continuous positive airway pressure. According to his pulmonologist, his sleep has markedly improved with this treatment, and his pulmonary function is within normal limits while using continuous positive airway pressure. He does not currently smoke cigarettes; he quit after his myocardial infarction. His current medications are aspirin, 81 mg daily; a calcium and vitamin D supplement; furosemide, 20 mg daily; metoprolol, 100 mg daily; simvastatin, 20 mg daily; and a multivitamin daily.

On physical examination, weight is 338 lb (153.6 kg) and height is 70.5 in (179.1 cm) (BMI = 48.0 kg/m²). Pulse rate is 68 beats/min, and blood pressure is 110/71 mm Hg. He has lower-extremity edema and hyperpigmentation consistent with venous stasis. The rest of the examination findings are normal.

As you discuss bariatric surgery with this patient, which of the following aspects of his history and physical examination is associated with higher perioperative mortality?
items.explanation:
## 87 ANSWER: D) History of deep venous thrombosis

87 Bariatric surgery has become an accepted treatment for the management of medically complicated obesity. The potential benefits regarding weight-related comorbidities such as type 2 diabetes, obstructive sleep apnea, and others are well recognized. Equally important is recognition of the risk factors associated with higher perioperative morbidity and mortality. Patients should fully understand their risks and physicians must recognize opportunities for intervention that can modify the risks.

The most common cause of perioperative mortality associated with bariatric surgery is thromboembolic disease, particularly pulmonary embolism. This patient has a history of deep venous thrombosis (Answer D). The second most common cause of death is anastomotic leaks. For patients who develop an anastomotic leak, the risk of pulmonary embolism rises significantly. The two most recognized risk factors associated with a higher morbidity and mortality are a history of deep venous thrombosis or pulmonary embolism and untreated obstructive sleep apnea (thus, Answer A is incorrect). Impaired functional status with the inability to walk 200 feet has consistently been shown to increase perioperative risk. Extreme BMI values are also associated with higher perioperative risks; however, the definition has varied among studies from BMI values greater than 50 kg/m² to values greater than 60 kg/m² (thus, Answer C is incorrect). A history of cigarette smoking (Answer E) has not been identified as a strong risk factor for higher perioperative mortality; however, it can increase the risk for anastomotic complications. Thus, smoking cessation is recommended. Among comorbidities, a history of coronary artery disease (Answer B) has not been associated with increased morbidity and mortality.

##### Educational Objective:

Advise patients contemplating bariatric surgery regarding risk factors for higher perioperative mortality.

UpToDate Topic Review(s):

Short-term medical outcomes following bariatric surgery

##### Reference(s):

The Longitudinal Assessment of Bariatric Surgery (LABS) Consortium. Perioperative safety in the longitudinal assessment of bariatric surgery. N Engl J Med. 2009;361(5):445-454.

Finks JF, English WJ, Carlin AM, et al; Michigan Bariatric Surgery Collaborative: Center for Healthcare Outcomes and Policy. Predicting risk for venous thromboembolism with bariatric surgery: results from the Michigan Bariatric Surgery Collaborative. Ann Surg. 2012;255(6):1100-1104.

items.question:
A 48-year-old man presents to discuss cardiovascular risk. He has treated hypertension, he smokes cigars occasionally (but not cigarettes), and he describes having a high-stress life. He works 60 to 80 hours per week, eats most of his meals at restaurants, and struggles to find time to exercise. His father died in a car crash at age 35 years, but his mother is alive and healthy and takes no medications. However, several paternal male relatives (2 uncles and his grandfather) had cardiovascular events in their 40s and 50s. The patient's primary care physician has told him that he should be on a statin, but he has seen advertisements for a variety of tests that can assess cardiovascular risk, and he wants to know if he really needs medication. He asks which tests he should have.

On physical examination, height is 74 in (188 cm) and weight is 250 lb (113.6 kg) (BMI = 32.1 kg/m $ ^{2} $). Blood pressure is 128/74 mm Hg. The rest of his examination findings are normal.

Laboratory test results (fasting):

Total cholesterol = 264 mg/dL (6.84 mmol/L)

LDL cholesterol = 160 mg/dL (4.14 mmol/L)

HDL cholesterol = 41 mg/dL (1.06 mmol/L)

Triglycerides = 189 mg/dL (2.14 mmol/L)

You enter these results into a risk calculator and conclude that he falls into the intermediate-risk category.

Which of the following is the most cost-effective test to order for this patient?
options: Measurement of apolipoprotein B or LDL particle number
items.explanation:
## 110 ANSWER: E) Measurement of apolipoprotein B or LDL particle number

Although laboratory and clinical parameter-based assessments of cardiovascular risk have been validated in a wide range of populations, they are not perfect. Individuals who have low predicted risk might still experience a cardiovascular event; conversely, individuals who have high predicted risk may never have an event, even when risk-reducing therapies (such as lipid-lowering therapies) are not used. Thus, there is an ongoing interest in additional means to assess cardiovascular risk, especially in situations such as the one described in this vignette—this patient scores at “intermediate” risk using the Framingham risk calculator.

Apolipoprotein B (Answer E) is the main structural protein for atherogenic lipoproteins (VLDL, IDL, LDL), but it is not present in HDL. There is 1 apolipoprotein B molecule per lipoprotein particle, so apolipoprotein B indicates particle number, whereas the LDL cholesterol indicates the amount of cholesterol found in the LDL fraction. An LDL-cholesterol concentration of 100 mg/dL (2.59 mmol/L) could be due to many smaller particles (with less cholesterol per particle) or fewer larger particles (with proportionately more cholesterol per particle). Because the smaller, denser LDL particles are thought to be more atherogenic than larger, more buoyant particles.

there has been an emerging interest in measuring particle number, and several commercial labs provide this service. The INTERHEART study was a large international study of cardiovascular risk in which apolipoprotein B levels were found to be a significant predictor of myocardial infarction, even after adjusting for age and traditional lipid risk factors. However, other studies have refuted this finding, so it remains controversial. There is potential redundancy because measurement of apolipoprotein B or LDL particle number can be approximated by the use of the non-HDL-cholesterol concentration: if the non-HDL-cholesterol level is more than 30 mg/dL (0.78 mmol/L) above the LDL-cholesterol level, this indicates high particle number. Some guidelines have recommended measurement of apolipoprotein B or LDL particle number, particularly in patients at high risk, to enhance risk assessment and provide apolipoprotein B goals for therapy. Although routine measurement of apolipoprotein B or LDL particle number has not been uniformly adopted, there is some suggestion in the literature that determining these values may be appropriate in individuals at very high risk, in those with hypertriglyceridemia, or in those with genetic dyslipidemias. Although cost-effectiveness for mass screening has not been formally assessed for apolipoprotein B measurement, it is relatively low-cost assay and is well validated in several populations. In this patient with elevated triglycerides, it may more accurately assess risk than measurement of LDL cholesterol or non-HDL cholesterol, and it is the best choice of the options presented here. However, it is important to note that measurement of apolipoprotein B or LDL particle number is not presently part of most guidelines. In fact, recent studies suggest that up to 75% of cholesterol laboratory testing could be avoided in a cost-effective strategy of cardiovascular risk assessment via a multistage screening approach using non-laboratory risk factors such as age, smoking status, blood pressure, and BMI.

The coronary artery calcium (CAC) score (Answer A) is determined by a CT-based scan that detects calcium, which indicates atherosclerosis in asymptomatic individuals. It has been proposed as a tool to detect disease (and thus risk for cardiovascular events) in persons who may be incorrectly assessed as being at low risk by traditional risk factors such as the Framingham score. Meta-analyses of CAC studies have found correlations between CAC scores and cardiac outcomes, with a relative risk of a cardiac event of 4.4 if CAC is present vs absent. As the C

116 ANSWER: C) Decrease vitamin D intake to 800 IU daily. Maintaining adequate calcium and vitamin D intake is a cornerstone in the management and prevention of osteoporosis. The current recommended intake for calcium and vitamin D for women with postmenopausal osteoporosis is 1200 mg daily and 800 IU daily, respectively. With discontinuation of the additional vitamin D₃ tablet, this patient's intake will be consistent with these dosages (thus, Answer C is correct and Answer E is incorrect). The patient's total daily intake of elemental calcium is 1200 mg, so Answers A and B are incorrect. No studies have clearly determined the optimal intake for calcium and vitamin D in premenopausal women. The current recommendations are 1000 mg daily of calcium and 600 IU daily of vitamin D.

Several over-the-counter vitamin D preparations are available. Although this wealth of choices and access facilitates recommended supplementation, it also increases the risk for toxicity when patients are unknowingly consuming excessive vitamin D. The Institute of Medicine defines the safe upper limit of vitamin D supplementation in healthy adults to be 4000 IU daily. Thus, this patient's current dosage should be reduced. Higher dosages put patients at risk for adverse effects and toxicity, so recognizing when a patient is taking excessive vitamin D supplementation is important. The biologic activity of vitamin  $ D_{3} $ may be slightly greater than that of  $ D_{2} $. Switching to 50,000 IU weekly of vitamin  $ D_{2} $ (Answer D) would most likely result in higher vitamin D levels and is thus not indicated.

Historically, obesity has been considered to be protective against fractures. However, a high prevalence of vitamin D deficiency has been described among obese persons, and this may potentially affect bone health. The beneficial effects of calcium and vitamin D supplementation on bone mineral density and fracture risk remains an active topic of investigation. Findings from several studies support a role for calcium and vitamin D supplementation in protecting against bone loss, but the effect on fracture risk is less clear. Most such studies have been conducted in postmenopausal women older than 50 years.

##### Educational Objective:

Advise patients regarding currently recommended vitamin D supplementation.

items.question: A 19-year-old man with type 1 diabetes (BMI 40.4 kg/m²), retinopathy, smokes 1 pack/day, LDL 186 mg/dL, HDL 26 mg/dL, TG 229 mg/dL, HbA1c 7.8%. In addition to smoking cessation, which intervention most reduces lifetime cardiovascular risk?
items.explanation: Despite young age, he has high lifetime ASCVD risk from T1DM, smoking, and marked dyslipidemia; statins provide the largest evidence-based risk reduction among listed options.
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Case 4: "Reduce cardiovascular risk in a young diabetic adult at high risk."

items.title: Why answer C is correct
items.text: Despite young age, he has high lifetime ASCVD risk from T1DM, smoking, and marked dyslipidemia; statins provide the largest evidence-based risk reduction among listed options.
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Case 4: "Reduce cardiovascular risk in a young diabetic adult at high risk."

items.title: How to apply this case clinically
items.text: In T1DM youth with LDL near 190 mg/dL and multiple risk factors, do not defer statin therapy while awaiting perfect glycemic control.
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Case 4: "Reduce cardiovascular risk in a young diabetic adult at high risk."

items.question: A 40-year-old woman (BMI 23.5 kg/m²) with 37% body fat on DXA, active lifestyle, family history of T2DM. Which pattern best describes her BMI status and cardiovascular risk?
items.explanation: Normal BMI with elevated body fat percentage confers metabolic risk—so-called normal-weight obesity—particularly in women.
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Case 19: "Interpret body composition data in assessing cardiometabolic risk."

items.title: Why answer C is correct
items.text: Normal BMI with elevated body fat percentage confers metabolic risk—so-called normal-weight obesity—particularly in women.
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Case 19: "Interpret body composition data in assessing cardiometabolic risk."

items.title: How to apply this case clinically
items.text: When BMI is normal but adiposity is high, assess body composition; metabolic risk may be underestimated by BMI alone.
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Case 19: "Interpret body composition data in assessing cardiometabolic risk."

items.statement: A statin is the most impactful listed therapy to lower lifetime ASCVD risk in a young T1DM smoker with LDL ~186 mg/dL.
items.explanation: Statin evidence for event reduction exceeds diet or fibrate monotherapy in this vignette.
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Case 4: "statin therapy is the most likely to have the greatest influence"

items.statement: A normal BMI excludes increased cardiometabolic risk.
items.explanation: Elevated body fat % with normal BMI still increases risk.
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Case 19: "normal BMI, increased adiposity confers an increased risk"

items.statement: Lifestyle change alone is sufficient cardiovascular therapy in high-risk dyslipidemic youth.
items.explanation: Lifestyle is necessary but often insufficient alone when LDL is very high.
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Case 4: "they are not sufficient by themselves"

items.statement: Fibrates reliably lower LDL cholesterol by >30%.
items.explanation: Fibrates mainly lower TG and raise HDL; LDL effects are minimal or upward.
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Case 4: "minimal effects on his LDL-cholesterol level"

items.statement: Low resting energy expenditure on calorimetry can indicate under-replaced hypothyroidism in panhypopituitarism.
items.explanation: Hypothyroidism reduces metabolic rate; levothyroxine adjustment can aid weight loss.
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Case 25: "values less than 80% of predicted are consistent with a hypometabolic state"

items.statement: Grapefruit juice can raise statin levels and toxicity risk.
items.explanation: CYP3A4 inhibition increases statin exposure.
items.reference: ESAP 2015: "Grapefruit juice"

items.statement: Apolipoprotein B may better capture atherogenic particle burden than LDL-C alone.
items.explanation: ApoB reflects particle number.
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Case 110: "Apolipoprotein B"

items.statement: Bariatric surgery can increase testosterone in obese hypogonadal men.
items.explanation: Weight loss reverses functional hypogonadism.
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Case 2: "Weight loss after gastric bypass surgery is associated with a significant increase in testosterone"

items.assertion: Intensified glucose control is the best next step when HbA1c is already ~7.8% in a young T1DM patient.
items.reason: Further glycemic tightening offers limited incremental CV benefit when already near intensive-trial levels.
items.explanation: A false; R explains limited benefit.
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Case 4: "improving his glycemic control now is not likely to substantially reduce his cardiovascular disease risk"

items.assertion: DXA body fat >35% in a woman with BMI 23.5 carries no added metabolic risk.
items.reason: Normal-weight obesity increases metabolic complications and mortality.
items.explanation: A false.
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Case 19: "increased adiposity confers an increased risk"

items.assertion: Niacin is preferred over statins in young insulin-resistant patients.
items.reason: Niacin worsens insulin resistance; statins have stronger outcome data.
items.explanation: A false.
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Case 4: "worsening of insulin resistance"

items.assertion: Increasing testosterone dose promotes weight loss in panhypopituitarism.
items.reason: Testosterone improves body composition but does not drive weight loss as primary therapy.
items.explanation: A false.
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Case 25: "this intervention alone is not expected to contribute to weight loss"

items.assertion: Lifetime risk is irrelevant when 10-year ASCVD risk is low in a 19-year-old.
items.reason: Elevated LDL in youth predicts lifetime events; treatment may still be warranted.
items.explanation: A false.
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Case 4: "lifetime risk is high"

items.assertion: Fibrate therapy is first-line when LDL is 186 mg/dL and TG 229 mg/dL in T1DM.
items.reason: Statin is prioritized for high LDL; fibrate role is mainly for severe hypertriglyceridemia.
items.explanation: A false.
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Case 4: "statin therapy"

items.assertion: Waist circumference adds no information when BMI is calculated.
items.explanation: A false.
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Case 19: "Waist circumference identifies individuals with abdominal adiposity"

items.assertion: Statins are contraindicated in all patients with chronic kidney disease.
items.reason: Statins are generally indicated in CKD for ASCVD prevention with dose adjustment.
items.explanation: A false.
items.reference: ESAP 2015: "Chronic kidney disease"
